title: A refined thermodynamic analysis of nonsecular master equations
authors: Mohamed Boubakour, Talia Szikman, Cyril Elouard
abstract: We present a systematic thermodynamic analysis of nonsecular master equations. We consider master equations resulting either from the partial secular and the geometric-arithmetic approximations, two approximations ensuring the positivity of the system's dynamics when some of its transition frequencies are too small to enable the full secular approximation. Both cause the system to relax towards a steady state which is not the Gibbs state of its bare Hamiltonian. Nonetheless, we build a unified, consistent thermodynamic framework for those dynamics. Starting from a microscopic expression of the second law based on system-environment correlations, we employ a systematic perturbation theory to preserve the positivity of the second law despite the approximations done on the dynamics. We show that, in spite of the weak system-bath coupling, the system-bath interaction energy participates to the energy balance, as well as the Lamb-shift. Those extra contributions give rise to work performed by the system on the bath when the former is out of equilibrium. We compare this microscopic entropy production with the definition based on the contractivity of the reduced system dynamics (Spohn inequality). We show that, unlike for secular master equations, the two entropy production rates differ because of the presence of non-vanishing stationary coherences in the energy eigenbasis. However, in the case of a single thermal bath, the difference is purely transient, and no work can be cyclically extracted from the steady-state despite its non-Gibbs form. Finally, we illustrate our results with a simple example, clarifying and completing the thermodynamic picture of Markovian dynamics in the quantum regime.
summary: This paper establishes a consistent thermodynamic framework for analyzing complex, nonsecular master equations describing open quantum systems. It systematically compares different physical definitions of entropy production and energy flow, showing how transient coherent effects modify standard predictions derived from simpler secular approximations. The work provides crucial insights into the limitations and necessary corrections when modeling real-world quantum dissipation.
main_problem: The paper develops a unified thermodynamic framework for analyzing nonsecular master equations, which arise when approximations are used that cause the system to relax toward a steady state different from the true Gibbs equilibrium.
main_result: It shows that while two definitions of entropy production (microscopic vs. Spohn inequality) differ due to transient coherent effects in nonsecular dynamics, no net work can be extracted cyclically from the steady state when coupled to a single thermal bath.
method: The analysis employs systematic perturbation theory starting from a microscopic second law based on system-environment correlations, comparing results derived from different approximations like Geometric-Arithmetic Master Equations (GAME) and Local Nonsecular Master Equations (LNME).
model_or_system: The model is an open quantum system coupled to thermal reservoirs (baths), described by master equations that go beyond the standard secular Lindblad form. The analysis compares dynamics derived from Born-Markov approximations.
key_observables: Entropy production rates ($\dot{\sigma}_{tot}$ vs $\dot{\sigma}_{Sp}$), energy balance components (system, bath, interaction energy), and stationary coherences in the energy eigenbasis.
important_parameters: Weak system-bath coupling regime; time scales related to bath correlation decay ($	au_B$) versus system relaxation ($\gamma^{-1}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on approximations such as the Born-Markov approximation and specific limiting procedures (e.g., deriving GAME from CGME).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate comparisons of heat flux components across different master equation regimes (local vs. full secular) and show the evolution of stationary coherence.
paper_structure: The paper builds by first establishing the Coarse-Grained Master Equation (CGME), then refining it through approximations like GAME, and finally performing a detailed thermodynamic comparison between microscopic entropy production and definitions based on system dynamics inequalities.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for any theoretical physicist studying open quantum systems, as it rigorously addresses the breakdown of standard secular approximations and provides necessary corrections to the first and second laws in non-equilibrium settings.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. An illustrative example of a quantum system where the PSA is relevant. Here we have two qubits that interact such that the coupling strength is very small compared to the frequencies. The spectrum will thus contain two close eigenstates such that we can identify H(0) S with the dominant Bohr frequencies ±ω and the perturbation δHS with Bohr frequencies ±2Ω satisfying Ω≪ω.

Figure 2. Total entropy production as a function of time. The blue curves correspond to the entropy obtained with GAME, the red ones to the correct entropy for the LNME and the green ones to the naive entropy production. The parameters are ω = 50JL(ω), δ = 10−3ω, βRω = 1, βL = βR/0.99, cLΛ = 0.5ω such that ΣL(ω) = 2.5JL(ω), and µ = 10−2βLg. The panel (a) shows the results obtained with cR = cL and g = 5 ∗10−3ω. Panel (b) corresponds to cR = cL and g = 10−2ω. Panel (c) corresponds to cR = 0.9cL and g = 10−2ω.

Figure 3. From (a) to (c): stationary heat flux of the right bath as a function of g for three different choices of cutoff and dissipation strength of the baths. The blue curves correspond to the results obtained with GAME, the red ones to the consistent description with the LNME and the orange ones to the heat flux in the full secular regime. The parameters are ω = 50JL(ω), δ = 10−3ω, βRω = 1, βL = βR/0.99. The panel (a) shows the results obtained with cLΛ = 0.2ω and cR = cL. Panel (b) corresponds to cLΛ = 0.5ω and cR = cL.
